I used to be an engineer for 10 years. It was so fun just to sit there in flow coding. You know this feeling. Just like, 'Oh, wow, it's working. Look at it compile. It's amazing. I'm making progress.' And now it's like you don't do that anymore. You just sit there and wait for agents to build the thing. What else is kind of lost in this new world for engineers?
turns.zh: 这些见解真的很有趣。随着角色转变，有得也有失。我好奇在软件工程的新世界里还失去了什么。我曾经做了 10 年工程师。坐在那里心流编码非常有趣。你知道那种感觉：‘哦，它工作了。看它编译。太棒了。我在进步。’现在你不再那样做了。你只是坐着等智能体构建东西。工程师在这个新世界里还失去了什么？

turns.en: Yeah, it's so funny. I just had that conversation with an engineer on the team about flow. Remember the old days you have this really gnarly problem and you pop that music soundtrack in and then you're just in the zone. So yeah, there is a little, and then there was always that big aha moment at the end. You remember you're like you finally cracked it. So we do, but I think now we get a lot of joy from the product. But I do hear from other engineers as well, some of the hardest parts is what I used to enjoy. So I think we're all just kind of shifting. But I do see the thing most, and that's why I was talking about the pairwise programming and the hackathon.
turns.zh: 是啊，真有趣。我刚刚和团队里一个工程师聊过心流。记得以前，你遇到一个棘手的问题，放上音乐，然后就进入状态了。所以有点怀念，最后总有一个大‘啊哈’时刻。你记得你终于搞定了。现在我们从产品中获得很多快乐。但我也听其他工程师说，一些最困难的部分正是我以前享受的。所以我觉得我们都在转变。但我最看重的是，这就是为什么我提到结对编程和黑客马拉松。

turns.en: That did recently come up more of folks were starting to feel like it's starting to be a lonely experience.
turns.zh: 最近确实越来越多的人开始觉得这开始变成一种孤独的体验。

turns.en: So interesting. Within Anthropic I'm curious so engineering like the most transformed I think of any role right now. What other What's like the second most transformed role so far would you say within Anthropic that's most different from how it was like a couple years ago let's say?
turns.zh: 真有意思。在 Anthropic 内部，我很好奇，工程是目前我认为变化最大的角色。那么，你觉得到目前为止，Anthropic 内部变化第二大的角色是什么？和几年前相比最不一样的是哪个？

turns.en: It's all the coding adjacent roles are shifting. Like PM is I know like you you were chatting with Kai. I think PM is also transformed quite a bit because PM bar no longer bottlenecked if if they have an idea waiting on engineering bandwidth. Um so that's kind of like the the next role I've seen shift. Like actually our PMs have also helped us roll up sleeves and help shift you know like some features when we when we were um you know like when there wasn't uh when there was a you know something we wanted to do and an engineer wasn't able to. So I think um it's all the coding adjacent roles are starting to shift. Um but I think that's where again the verification is important cuz when you have more different disciplines checking in how do we make sure everybody has high confidence. I also think we need to do more to keep automating these other portions of the workflows. Like for sure we focus a lot on coding but next when you think about like design or data science like those are starting to be the next areas I think are good opportunities for us to see how we can yeah like um uh start and improving kind of like experiences there as well.
turns.zh: 所有与编码相关的角色都在转变。比如产品经理，我知道你和 Kai 聊过。我认为产品经理也发生了很大变化，因为产品经理不再受限于等待工程资源来实现想法。所以这算是下一个我看到转变的角色。实际上，我们的产品经理也卷起袖子帮忙推动一些功能，比如当我们想做某件事但工程师无法完成时。所以我认为所有与编码相关的角色都开始转变。但这也凸显了验证的重要性，因为当更多不同学科的人参与进来时，我们如何确保每个人都有很高的信心？我还认为我们需要做更多工作来持续自动化工作流程的其他部分。当然我们很关注编码，但接下来，想想设计或数据科学，这些开始成为下一个领域，我认为是很好的机会，看看我们如何也能开始改善那里的体验。

turns.en: Yeah, I have a speaking of data science, I have a data science friend and he was just saying how data science is so different now where now most of their job is people doing their own like not amazing data science work using AI and it and then just like showing it to the data scientist, "Here, here's the analysis I did. Can you just make sure it's right?" And half the time it's not right. And so the job is just like a whole different job now instead of doing the work that they thought they wanted to do and that's like, "All right, I'm just reviewing all this AI data science all the time. What the hell?"
turns.zh: 说到数据科学，我有一个数据科学朋友，他说现在数据科学完全不同了，大部分工作变成了别人用 AI 做自己的数据科学工作（通常做得不怎么样），然后拿给数据科学家看：“这是我做的分析，你能确认一下对不对吗？”而一半时间都是错的。所以现在的工作完全变了，不再是他们原本想做的，而是“好吧，我整天就在审核这些 AI 数据科学的东西，搞什么鬼？”

turns.en: Kind of along the lines, coming back to just how your team operates and how things are changing, let me see if something comes with this question. What should an engineering manager expect from their team now versus like a couple years ago? What's like a normal, I don't know, baseline expectation of how things should work? Is it other than just, you know, we're shipping faster? Is there anything else?
turns.zh: 顺着这个思路，回到你的团队运作方式和变化，我想问：与几年前相比，现在工程经理应该对团队有什么期望？什么是正常的基线期望？除了我们交付更快之外，还有其他变化吗？

turns.en: Oh, interesting. Well, definitely I think most commits are cloud assisted and so that was a shift. Um I think you know, I kind of like mentioned we have like Slack channels with all the feedback and also our dashboards that we have get to cloud. I think having engineers building like keep building that stronger product sense muscle is I I think also like another and I think that in general helps kind of build these really strong minded product engineers. I would say more of these roles that were traditionally non-engineering, you do now see engineers being like and sometimes you're just blocked by, you know, waiting for you know, a cross-functional partner. I think there's there's less of those blocks now just because the models are able to augment additional capabilities that you may not have as an from as an engineer.
turns.zh: 哦，有意思。嗯，我认为大多数提交都有云端辅助，这是一个转变。我们有一些 Slack 频道收集所有反馈，还有仪表盘也迁移到云端。我认为让工程师持续培养更强的产品感，这有助于打造真正有想法的产品工程师。我注意到，以前很多非工程角色，现在工程师也能胜任，有时你不再被跨职能伙伴卡住。现在这些阻碍变少了，因为模型能够增强工程师原本不具备的额外能力。

turns.en: So it's kind of interesting that it's both directions. Engineers becoming more product minded and responsible for the quality and success of a product and then everybody becoming an engineer more and more.
turns.zh: 所以有趣的是，这是双向的。工程师变得更有产品思维，对产品的质量和成功负责，而每个人也变得越来越像工程师。

turns.en: Yeah, that's right. It's all blurring.
turns.zh: 对，没错。一切都在模糊化。

turns.en: Yeah. I forget who said this, but there's this like like what is a role anymore in this this guy said that it's like what's the average of what you do? What's like the highest percentage of what you and that's kind of your role now, whatever it is going to be.
turns.zh: 是啊。我忘了谁说的，但有个说法是：角色到底是什么？这个人说，就是你做的事情的平均值？你做得最多的那件事，那就是你现在的角色，不管它是什么。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 嗯。

turns.en: Oh man, I want to come back to your point about obsession with the the product living breathing the product dog fooding. I talked to a bunch of people that work with you about you and that's the thing that came up most just like your obsession with living and breathing the product using the product constantly, whatever it is you're building uh this idea of dog fooding. Talk about just like why that is so important, why that's something you instill within your teams and and and reports.
turns.zh: 哦，我想回到你刚才说的对产品的痴迷，就是亲身使用产品、吃自己的狗粮。我和很多与你共事的人聊过你，他们提到最多的就是你痴迷于亲身使用产品，无论你在构建什么，都不断使用它。说说为什么这如此重要，为什么你要向你的团队和下属灌输这一点。

turns.en: Oh, I love it. Um yeah, I think it's it's really and and this has worked for me um it's just been a really good way for me to keep a pulse on you know, anytime you build product there's there's a dream. Like you're really hoping to enable an experience or make an experience better. So I think um being able like like that helps me keep really close to the pulse. I also think uh maybe and maybe something like for sure on Visual Studio that was like, you know, where I where I got this love of it um but it's interesting cuz even Marketplace, I remembered every time once in a while I'll do like even after I left the team actually. Um one time I had like a MacBook Air and I wanted to sell my old and you know, I haven't sold anything on Marketplace let let me and I could not believe it the minute I I put it up for sale a seller or buyer tried to scam me and it was an interesting like new scam vector I didn't detect and so but that goes to again like people will use uh your products in ways that that you may not expect and so especially as leaders or even like anyone on the team, we all have different like life like actually it's it's funny when it when I was a you know, supporting the VR and AR teams, somehow how I used VR, the setup, I would always be able to find these really weird floor height issues. So, that ended up being a I took a you know, like I'm going to help us you know, like cuz somehow I got a good repro environment. Um And so, I think it's number one like making that's how you keep your pulse on the product that you're building and don't get too lost in metrics and dashboards only or presentations.
turns.zh: 哦，我很喜欢这个。嗯，这对我来说真的很有效，是保持对产品脉搏感知的好方法。任何时候你构建产品，都有一个梦想——你希望实现某种体验或让体验变得更好。所以这能让我非常贴近脉搏。另外，可能是在 Visual Studio 上，我培养了对这种做法的热爱。但有趣的是，即使是 Marketplace，我离开团队后偶尔还会用。有一次我有一台 MacBook Air，想卖掉旧的，我之前没在 Marketplace 上卖过东西。结果我一挂上去，就有买家试图骗我，这是一个我没发现的新骗局。这说明人们会以你意想不到的方式使用你的产品。所以作为领导者或团队成员，我们都有不同的生活经历。有趣的是，当我支持 VR 和 AR 团队时，我使用 VR 设置总能发现一些奇怪的楼层高度问题。这最终帮助了团队，因为我有一个很好的复现环境。所以我认为，第一，这是保持对产品脉搏感知的方式，不要只沉迷于指标、仪表盘或演示。

turns.en: I think that's such an important point you're saying there that I just want to make sure people hear is just like like there's always this idea of anecdotal evidence and just like examples versus the data. And what you're saying here is as a product leader as an engine leader, there you found a lot of success in like the anecdotes, the specific uh little one-offs that you experience as a as a as a user versus like obsessing with the data only.
turns.zh: 我觉得你说的这一点非常重要，我想确保大家听到：总是存在轶事证据和例子与数据之间的对比。而你说的是，作为产品领导者和工程领导者，你在轶事、那些作为用户经历的特定小案例上取得了很大成功，而不是只沉迷于数据。

turns.en: Yeah. And and actually, sometimes it's also how I've been able to most effectively help the team.
turns.zh: 是的。实际上，有时这也是我能够最有效帮助团队的方式。

transcript.sec: Using the product and customer visits

turns.en: So, for example, the last team I was on, I was leading a VR team. I had not checked in any code into that codebase because I was really worried about messing up the operating system. But we were doing a lot of polish fixes, and I wanted to use my dogfooding time to actually vet how the experience looks. That was also a way I felt I could still meaningfully contribute to help hold the quality bar for the team. Every team member usually really appreciates that because as a leader, you're supporting folks on the team. Outside of metrics, everybody really wants to make sure their work matters. Just making sure that leaders use your product, I think folks feel the leaders remain really engaged.
turns.zh: 举个例子，我上一个团队是领导一个VR团队。我没有向那个代码库提交任何代码，因为我真的很担心搞乱操作系统。但我们做了很多打磨修复，我想利用自己的‘吃狗粮’时间来实际检验体验效果。这也是我觉得自己还能有意义地贡献、帮助团队保持质量门槛的一种方式。每个团队成员通常都很感激，因为作为领导者，你在支持团队里的人。在指标之外，每个人都希望自己的工作有意义。确保领导者使用你的产品，我觉得大家会感到领导者仍然非常投入。

turns.en: And this connects a lot with your point that engineers need to become more product minded. Engineers need to become more PM-y, PMs need to become more... But as an engineer, this is one way to do it: use the product constantly. That'll help you understand what is missing in the product as a user because you're just using it.
turns.zh: 这和你说的工程师需要更有产品思维的观点很相关。工程师需要更像产品经理，产品经理需要更像……但作为工程师，这是做到这一点的一种方式：不断使用产品。这会帮助你作为用户理解产品缺少什么，因为你就在使用它。

turns.en: That's right. And I would say if you're leading a team where it's really hard for you to use the product, then meet with customers. Whatever the other avenue is, I think that's also been really important. Every time I've done customer visits, I always learn something new. I remember when we were trying to launch Facebook Marketplace to Latin America, we were testing in Chile. It just wasn't doing as well as the other regions. We had a really small research trip where I went to Chile. I got a whole bunch of Android phones. It was a very small crew, just three of us. Upon landing, I opened up these Android phones and realized the LTE connection was much slower than what we were used to in the US. The Marketplace feed didn't even load very well on those little LTE situations. What a growth blocker—you can't even load. That's why it's so important to always listen to customer feedback and get that fast feedback loop.
turns.zh: 没错。我想说，如果你领导一个团队，很难亲自使用产品，那就去见客户。无论其他途径是什么，我认为这也很重要。每次我做客户拜访，总能学到新东西。我记得我们试图在拉丁美洲推出Facebook Marketplace时，在智利做测试。效果不如其他地区。我们组织了一次很小的调研之旅，我去了智利。我拿了一堆安卓手机。团队很小，只有三个人。落地后，我打开这些安卓手机，发现LTE连接比我们在美国习惯的要慢得多。Marketplace的信息流在那种慢速LTE环境下加载得很差。这真是个增长障碍——连加载都不行。这就是为什么始终倾听客户反馈并建立快速反馈循环如此重要。

turns.en: I think it was Jeff Bezos that said if you have the data and you have an anecdote, trust the anecdote over the data. Surprisingly. And that's a great example. Okay, just a couple more questions. One is in your talk, you had this really interesting slide at the end of what questions you're kind of rethinking about right now that you haven't figured out how to solve with how much is changing. I'm going to read the three. I'm curious if it's still these three, if there's anything else like current problems we need to figure out that we haven't solved in how we operate. What you shared in your talk was: do we still need separate iOS and Android orgs? How far do you push fully automated reviews? And with role blurring, how do you ensure everyone's equally productive? Still problems? Is there anything else that you're thinking about like, okay, we need to crack this. We haven't figured this out.
turns.zh: 我记得杰夫·贝佐斯说过，如果你有数据和一个故事，相信故事而不是数据。令人惊讶。这是个很好的例子。好了，再问几个问题。在你的演讲中，最后有一张很有趣的幻灯片，列出了你正在重新思考的问题，这些问题你还没想好怎么解决，因为变化太大了。我来读一下这三个问题。我好奇的是，是否还是这三个问题，或者还有其他当前需要解决但尚未解决的运营问题。你在演讲中分享的是：我们还需要独立的iOS和Android组织吗？全自动审核要推进到什么程度？随着角色模糊化，如何确保每个人同样高效？这些问题还在吗？还有其他你正在思考的、需要攻克但还没想明白的问题吗？

turns.en: You know, the iOS Android one, I think we're still actually—it's funny because I speak of deep expertise. We definitely feel it's really important to still bring on folks with that expertise, but we probably don't need as large because people are flexing. So it's again making sure we have the Android and iOS experts, but less of a larger mobile org per platform. That's still a balance we're trying to figure out: do we have the right and enough expertise? The second one was—sorry, what was the second one?
turns.zh: 你知道，iOS和Android那个问题，我觉得我们其实还在——有趣的是，我提到了深度专长。我们确实觉得仍然需要引进有这些专长的人，但可能不需要那么大规模，因为人们在灵活变通。所以还是要确保我们有Android和iOS专家，但每个平台不需要那么大的移动组织。这仍然是我们试图平衡的问题：我们是否有正确且足够的专长？第二个问题是——抱歉，第二个是什么来着？

turns.en: Oh, yeah. It is how far do you push fully automated reviews?
turns.zh: 哦，对。是全自动审核要推进到什么程度？

turns.en: Ah, yes. Actually, well, this is a fun one. With the content design check, I think we're looking for across all of it: how do you actually get what good looks like? The verification is still one that I think there's a lot more opportunities there for us. But how far to push, I think looking at where we think that experts still matters, and then also have to keep asking ourselves, okay, is there a way to leverage our expertise to also automate? Looking across the whole end-to-end experience, making sure we're not missing things. We come at it from an engineering standpoint, but making sure experiences how we think about those other areas. I think there's definitely still more that we can do there.
turns.zh: 啊，对。这其实是个有趣的问题。关于内容设计检查，我认为我们在所有方面都在寻找：如何真正定义‘好’的样子？验证仍然是一个我认为还有很多机会的领域。但推进到什么程度，我认为要看我们觉得专家仍然重要的地方，然后还要不断问自己：有没有办法利用我们的专长来实现自动化？纵观整个端到端体验，确保我们没有遗漏。我们从工程角度出发，但也要确保体验如何考虑其他领域。我觉得我们肯定还能做得更多。

turns.en: Basically, how do you solve how do you set up a verification that the experience is what you want it to be?
turns.zh: 基本上，就是如何解决如何设置验证，确保体验符合你的期望？

turns.en: Exactly. That and I think that one is still a hard one to crack because you kind of mentioned the eval cases. Some of it for sure is accuracy, but it's also the experience. So that's something we're still kind of thinking through.
turns.zh: 正是。这个问题仍然很难解决，因为你提到了评估案例。部分肯定是准确性，但也关乎体验。所以我们还在思考。

turns.en: Awesome. Is there anything else that's like this has changed recently we got to figure out how to rethink the way we operate or is this kind of the big ones?
turns.zh: 太棒了。还有其他最近发生变化、我们需要重新思考运营方式的问题吗？还是主要就是这些？

turns.en: You know, I think because with routines and everything being more async, I think there is starting to be a high load on our context switching. I even remember I myself was like, oh I kicked off like I kicked off like... So I think that's probably another thing we have to think about: how do we, whether for our team members or our users, how can we actually make the experience better to reduce that load? Because I do see the context switching load increasing.
turns.zh: 你知道，因为日常工作和一切变得更加异步，我觉得上下文切换的负担开始变重。我甚至记得我自己也……所以我认为这可能是另一个我们需要思考的问题：我们如何为团队成员或用户，真正改善体验来减轻这种负担？因为我确实看到上下文切换的负担在增加。

turns.en: I can imagine if you have 20 agents running, there's just endless checking in and reviewing, and you have to remember what you're doing there. It's such an interesting world where the idea flow we talked about before—engineers and most people have less of the just hours of flow, but now the agents can kind of just remind you: here's where you're at. The reset to switch almost is easier because you don't have to relearn everything. You don't have to re-understand the codebase and the architecture. You can kind of just like, okay, but here's what we're trying to do. It's kind of like both got better, got worse.
turns.zh: 我可以想象，如果你有20个智能体在运行，就会有没完没了的检查和审查，你必须记住你在做什么。这是一个非常有趣的世界，我们之前谈到的‘心流’——工程师和大多数人很少有连续几小时的心流，但现在智能体可以提醒你：你当前的状态。切换时的重置几乎更容易，因为你不需要重新学习一切。你不需要重新理解代码库和架构。你可以直接说，好吧，但这是我们要做的。这有点像既变好了，也变坏了。

turns.en: Well, it's interesting because I used to block out focus time for coding, but now with more async agents, I find I need to block focus time just to catch up on all the async work I've kicked off.
turns.zh: 嗯，这很有趣，因为我以前会专门留出专注时间写代码，但现在有了更多异步智能体，我发现我需要再留出专注时间来跟进我发起的各种异步工作。

turns.en: Yeah, do you have any thoughts on a solution? That's hard. People just want to do more and more, but how do you do that without constant context switching? It's really hard and annoying.
turns.zh: 是啊，你有什么解决办法吗？这很难。人们就是想做越来越多的事，但怎么才能不频繁切换上下文呢？真的很烦人。

turns.en: Yes, I agree. I definitely haven't cracked it yet.
turns.zh: 是的，我同意。我肯定还没找到解决办法。

transcript.secZh: 上下文切换与专注时间

transcript.sec: AI and the Future of Engineering Jobs

turns.en: So interesting. There's a question I'm excited to hear your answer on. It's about jobs and hiring. You think AI would make engineers less necessary, but you guys are hiring like crazy. Where do you think this goes? What about the future of the engineer role?
turns.zh: 真有意思。有个问题我很想听听你的看法。是关于工作和招聘的。你觉得AI会让工程师变得不那么必要，但你们却在疯狂招人。你觉得这会怎么发展？工程师角色的未来是什么？

turns.en: I'll share some vulnerability here. One big open question is how we grow the next generation. How you and I got to our engineering path is so different. When you graduate from school, how do you fast-forward it? But the important thing to me is that they still understand the double-click down to the layer of an eight. I wonder if for software engineering, it's almost like a fellowship or apprenticeship program. I know internships are only three months with small projects, but I wonder how to cram in some of the life experiences we got and teach that to the next generation of builders.
turns.zh: 我来坦诚说一点。一个很大的开放问题是我们如何培养下一代。你和我走上工程道路的方式截然不同。当你从学校毕业，怎么加速这个过程？但对我来说重要的是，他们仍然理解深入到底层细节的那种“双击”。我想知道软件工程是不是更像一种研究员或学徒项目。我知道实习只有三个月，做小项目，但我想知道如何把我们获得的一些人生经验压缩进去，教给下一代建设者。

turns.en: Right, if you never have to look at code, what's the incentive for a new software engineer to truly understand how infrastructure works, memory allocation, and all those foundational things?
turns.zh: 没错，如果你从来不需要看代码，那新软件工程师有什么动力去真正理解基础设施如何工作、内存分配以及所有那些基础的东西呢？

turns.en: And it's interesting. Maybe the models will get good enough that it doesn't matter, but I do think there's something about that double-click because that's where there might be an opportunity to improve the product or system. But figuring out how to learn that not necessarily by years of typing code.
turns.zh: 这很有趣。也许模型会变得足够好，以至于这都不重要了，但我确实认为那种深入理解是有意义的，因为那可能是改进产品或系统的机会。但如何学会这一点，不一定非得靠多年写代码。

turns.en: Yeah, somebody has to understand code at some level. It's like some COBOL engineer they have to pull out of retirement one day: 'Do you remember how to write Python?'
turns.zh: 是啊，总得有人在某个层面上理解代码。就像有些COBOL工程师，有一天不得不被从退休中拉出来：‘你还记得怎么写Python吗？’

turns.en: You know what was really fun? One of my previous managers started as a software engineer when it was punch cards. He's been messaging me everything he's been building with Claude Code. I think about his career: how he started with punch cards was totally different. It'll be interesting to see what remains important and what changes. Maybe the important things shift over time, and we need to build proficiency in them.
turns.zh: 你知道什么很有趣吗？我的一位前经理是从打孔卡时代开始做软件工程师的。他一直在给我发他用Claude Code构建的东西。想想他的职业生涯：他从打孔卡开始，完全不一样。看看什么仍然重要、什么会改变，会很有意思。也许重要的东西会随着时间推移而变化，我们需要建立相应的熟练度。

turns.en: Oh yeah, just learn the things that really matter. The argument I constantly hear is that it's a new level of abstraction, just like assembly and binary—it keeps going up. Now we don't need to look at the code; it's a new layer of abstraction: prompts and Claude's thinking messages.
turns.zh: 哦对，只学真正重要的东西。我经常听到的说法是，这是一个新的抽象层次，就像汇编和二进制一样——它不断上升。现在我们不需要看代码了；这是一个新的抽象层：提示词和Claude的思考消息。

turns.en: Yeah, and maybe it's about what is an interesting problem, what's the prioritization for experience to build. When you build things, how do you know it's resonating and doing what you intended?
turns.zh: 是啊，也许关键是什么是有趣的问题，什么经验值得优先积累。当你构建东西时，你怎么知道它引起了共鸣，并且达到了预期效果？

turns.en: And is it good? Are we building a bunch of slop or is this actually architecture that will work? I think the advantage for young people is it's so much easier to lean in and work in this new way versus being stuck in the old ways. It's amazing how many long-time engineers like yourself have adapted and embraced this. It's so hard to change everything.
turns.zh: 而且它好吗？我们是在建一堆垃圾，还是这真的是可行的架构？我认为年轻人的优势在于，他们更容易投入并以这种新方式工作，而不是固守旧方式。像你这样长期工作的工程师能适应并拥抱这一点，真是令人惊讶。改变一切太难了。

turns.en: Well, the rate of change is also so fast. I remember the first time I used Sonnet 3.5 or 3.6, it was still making mistakes. But some engineers resisting AI tooling would point out those mistakes. It was hard to understand the exponential rate of improvement. Maybe something I tried to automate wasn't good enough, but in the next model it was. So it's always worth revisiting things that didn't work, because now there might be a new capability.
turns.zh: 嗯，变化的速度也很快。我记得第一次用Sonnet 3.5或3.6时，它还会犯错。但一些抵制AI工具的工程师会指出这些错误。很难理解那种指数级的改进速度。也许我尝试自动化的事情还不够好，但在下一个模型里就好了。所以总是值得重新审视那些没成功的事情，因为现在可能有了新能力。

turns.en: Yeah, that comes up a lot in this podcast. Just build something that is almost working, at the edge, because once the model gets there, you'll be so far ahead. Okay, final question. You may have already answered this, but what keeps you up at night?
turns.zh: 是啊，这个播客里经常提到这一点。就构建那些几乎能用的、处于边缘的东西，因为一旦模型达到那个水平，你就会遥遥领先。好了，最后一个问题。你可能已经回答了，但什么让你夜不能寐？

turns.en: The thing that keeps me up at night is probably how we... we talked about Claude Code and co-work team culture.
turns.zh: 让我夜不能寐的可能是我们如何……我们谈到了Claude Code和协作团队文化。

turns.en: That's and the team culture is really important to me. Like it's the one team mentality and I share with folks. And by the way, culture is like a living breathing thing. It's not just a poster you slap on a wall and it changes over time and it shows up in how we treat each other, how we're there for each other. So, the culture of the team is important to me because we are growing and since the culture shifts, making sure that maintaining the things that are important, it's really important for me to have diverse perspectives so we can have healthy open honest debates in the open and we welcome those. And I call that one team mentality: when you get close to the finish line, look behind you and see if there's someone on our team to help, because we finish as a team. That's probably the thing that keeps me up at night. There are so many other hard problems, but I think a lot of the other ones are product or engineering challenges that we have dashboards or theories or hypotheses, but the culture is a human aspect. I think that's the one I always want to make sure that as we grow, we still maintain that culture because it is the fiber of the team. When it starts drifting, I'm always worried: if it drifts, are we catching it and having conversations as a team together to make sure we all want the culture to grow in the right direction?
turns.zh: 团队文化对我来说非常重要。这是一种“一个团队”的心态，我也经常和同事们分享。而且，文化是活生生的东西，不是贴在墙上的海报，它会随着时间变化，体现在我们如何对待彼此、如何互相支持。所以，团队文化对我来说很重要，因为我们在成长，文化也在变化。确保那些重要的东西得以保持，对我来说非常关键。我希望有多元化的视角，这样我们才能进行健康、开放、诚实的辩论，我们欢迎这些辩论。我称之为“一个团队”的心态：当你接近终点线时，回头看看是否有团队成员需要帮助，因为我们是一起完成任务的。这大概就是让我夜不能寐的事情。还有很多其他难题，但我觉得很多都是产品或工程上的挑战，我们有仪表盘、理论或假设，但文化是人的方面。我认为这是我一直想确保的：随着我们的成长，我们仍然保持那种文化，因为它是团队的根基。当它开始偏离时，我总是担心：如果它偏离了，我们是否及时发现了，并作为团队一起讨论，确保我们都希望文化朝着正确的方向发展？

turns.en: Yeah, I imagine everybody is struggling with this considering the pace of change and the pace of hiring, especially a company like Anthropic that's in this crazy, most unprecedented growth trajectory in history. I could see how that could be a challenge with so much change. It makes sense. Even at Airbnb when I was there, that was quite a growth trajectory and that was nothing like what you guys are going through. And that was a constant topic of conversation: how do we maintain the culture?
turns.zh: 是的，我想每个人都在为此挣扎，考虑到变化的速度和招聘的速度，尤其是像 Anthropic 这样的公司，正处在历史上最疯狂、最前所未有的增长轨道上。我能理解在这么多变化中这有多难。这很合理。即使在我待过的 Airbnb，那也是一个相当大的增长轨迹，但和你们经历的完全没法比。那也是一个持续讨论的话题：我们如何保持文化？

turns.en: Actually, I'm curious what was your experience at Airbnb to maintain the culture as you were growing?
turns.zh: 实际上，我很好奇你在 Airbnb 的经历，你们在成长过程中是如何保持文化的？

turns.en: One thing is just what worked well: the founders being obsessed with it. Every all-hands, every big meeting, just reminding of the culture and the value of the culture and what the values are. The founders top-down being obsessed with it was a really big part of it. It just couldn't have a meeting without that coming up. The other is a memory I always come back to: we had Sheryl Sandberg from Meta come to do a fireside chat and somebody asked her how do you maintain culture as you scale? Because we're going so fast and it's so hard to deal with all this change and culture and all these new people. Her advice was: this is actually the problem you want to have because this means you're growing and doing well. This is normal. Nothing will change if you're doing badly. That's a much worse situation when you're not growing and you're not hiring like crazy. That's a much worse situation that will cause even more suffering. So this is a good problem you're dealing with. That advice has always stuck with me.
turns.zh: 其中一个有效的做法是：创始人对文化非常执着。每次全员大会、每次大型会议，都会提醒大家文化和价值观。创始人自上而下地痴迷于此，这占了很大一部分。没有一次会议不提到这个。另一个我经常回忆起的记忆是：我们请来了 Meta 的 Sheryl Sandberg 做炉边谈话，有人问她如何在规模化时保持文化？因为我们发展太快，很难应对所有这些变化、文化和新人。她的建议是：这其实是你想要的问题，因为这表明你在成长，做得很好。这是正常的。如果你做得不好，什么都不会改变。那种不成长、不疯狂招人的情况要糟糕得多，会造成更多痛苦。所以你现在面对的是一个好问题。这个建议一直留在我心里。

turns.en: Oh, that's great. It's interesting. Here you talk, I think one of the important things to kind of cloud code and co-work team is whether ICs or managers, but this is a thing I especially ask for managers on the team: it's really important that we all talk about what's going well, but also just be open about what's not going well. Because if we can actually have a conversation about what's not going well, that's how we can actually address it. Speaking of what keeps me up at night, my nightmare is especially if someone's in a manager position and I'm like, 'Hey, how are things going?' and they say, 'Everything's fine.' I'm like, 'Oh my gosh, I'm not doing fine. I know things are not fine.' It's like that meme of the doctor cat with a cup of coffee in a room that's on fire. That is my nightmare. So that's actually a discussion I have with a lot of folks on the team, especially managers when they first join: 'Hey, let's always have these open conversations so that we can solve problems together.'
turns.zh: 哦，那太好了。这很有趣。你提到，我认为在 Claude Code 和协作团队中，重要的事情之一，无论是个人贡献者还是管理者，但我特别要求团队中的管理者：我们不仅要谈论进展顺利的事情，还要坦诚地谈论不顺利的事情。因为如果我们能真正讨论不顺利的事情，我们才能解决它。说到让我夜不能寐的事情，我的噩梦是，如果某人是管理者，我问‘嘿，事情进展如何？’他们回答‘一切都好。’我会想，‘天哪，我不好。我知道事情并不好。’这就像那个 meme：医生猫在着火的房间里喝咖啡。那就是我的噩梦。所以这实际上是我和团队中很多人，尤其是新加入的管理者讨论的话题：‘嘿，我们一定要保持这种开放的对话，这样我们才能一起解决问题。’

turns.en: I imagine there's something a lot of people struggle with: seeing so many people around them doing super well, at least seemingly doing well. Everything's going great. I'm growing this awesome company. It's hard to actually be honest and say it's not going great. I'm struggling here. I'm falling behind because everyone around you is just on the surface feeling like they're killing it.
turns.zh: 我想很多人都在挣扎：看到周围很多人做得非常好，至少表面上看起来很好。一切都很顺利。我在发展这家很棒的公司。很难真正诚实地说‘并不顺利，我在挣扎，我在落后’，因为周围每个人表面上都觉得自己干得很棒。

turns.en: Mhm.
turns.zh: 嗯。

turns.en: Yeah. Before we get to our very exciting lightning round, is there anything else that you either want to leave listeners with, anything else that we didn't cover, anything else that's important that you wanted to share?
turns.zh: 是的。在我们进入非常激动人心的快速问答环节之前，你还有什么想留给听众的吗？还有什么我们没有覆盖到的，或者你想分享的重要事情？

turns.en: Maybe one thing is just a suggestion. You know, we talked about how Claude can automate things. One other thing that's really big on Claude Code and Core team culture is explicit permission to kill processes that no longer serve us. So maybe a suggestion is for anyone on our work and our team or leading teams: take your, what's one process that you either dread doing or is really highly noisy or is really expensive in terms of a lot of manual work? Pick one thing and first ask: is it still serving its purpose? For example, even our planning. Actually, that was my own big first learning when I first joined Claude Code. I said, 'Hey, maybe we should do a six-month roadmap doc, but we're going to do it super lightweight because I don't want to waste a lot of time planning.' And I felt we did a really lightweight process. But that was such a good learning for me because the exercise was good to kickstart conversations and ensure we're aligned, but three months into it, I'm like, 'Wait, have we still referenced it?' Because so much has changed. And that was also something I changed to that I myself brought in, thinking, 'Hey, maybe this will help.' So always be open to learning and always ask yourself: whatever process you have, is it still serving its purpose? Just because the field is changing so fast.
turns.zh: 也许有一件事只是一个建议。你知道，我们讨论了 Claude 如何实现自动化。在 Claude Code 和核心团队文化中，另一件非常重要的事情是明确允许废除不再服务于我们的流程。所以，也许给任何在我们团队工作或领导团队的人一个建议：找出一个你害怕做、或者噪音很大、或者非常昂贵（比如很多手工工作）的流程。选一个，首先问：它还有存在的意义吗？例如，甚至我们的规划。实际上，那是我刚加入 Claude Code 时第一个重要的学习。我说，‘嘿，也许我们应该做一个六个月的路线图文档，但我们要做得非常轻量，因为我不想浪费太多时间做规划。’我觉得我们做了一个非常轻量的流程。但那对我来说是一个很好的学习，因为那个练习有助于启动对话并确保我们保持一致，但三个月后，我想，‘等等，我们还在参考它吗？’因为变化太大了。那也是我自己带来并改变的东西，想着‘嘿，也许这会有帮助。’所以，永远保持开放学习的心态，并经常问自己：你现有的流程，它还在服务于它的目的吗？因为领域变化太快了。

turns.en: I love that advice. I got to follow up on this real quick.
turns.zh: 我喜欢这个建议。我得快速跟进一下这个问题。

turns.en: So, what is it? How do you think about planning now? Do you do any planning? Is it just like a month-long road map? What's kind of like the simple way to explain where you're at with that?
turns.zh: 那么，你现在是怎么考虑规划的？你们做规划吗？是不是就像一个月度的路线图？简单来说，你们现在是怎么做的？

turns.en: Yeah, I call it JIT planning now, like just-in-time planning. So, it is around like because yeah, I think 6 months was too long. So, now for sure some projects will take more than a month, but we try to do like a month of planning, really lightweight. Actually, there's not even docs. It's really just us aligning on a little spreadsheet of what we think is important. But even that one, I'm kind of thinking through, 'Hey, like every week we should probably still keep a like what we're trying is here are the month's priorities.' And we're going to try it out, but I have a feeling like every week we'll probably want to do really quick like, 'Hey, just to check. Yep, this is still this month's priorities. Good.' Um but yeah, like now we've shrunk it to JIT monthly planning.
turns.zh: 是的，我现在称之为JIT规划，也就是即时规划。因为我觉得六个月太长了。所以现在，虽然有些项目确实会超过一个月，但我们尽量只做一个月规划，非常轻量。实际上，连文档都没有，我们只是在一个小表格里对齐一下我们认为重要的事情。但即使这样，我也在想，'嘿，也许我们每周还是应该确认一下这个月的优先级是什么。'我们打算试试看，但我感觉可能每周我们都会快速检查一下，'嘿，确认一下，这仍然是这个月的优先级。好的。'嗯，是的，现在我们把它缩减成了JIT月度规划。

turns.en: So, it's monthly meaning for the next month, here's a little sheet/Excel spreadsheet of what we're planning to do for the next month. And then every week check in, is this still what we're planning to do for the next month?
turns.zh: 所以，月度规划就是，对于下个月，我们有一个小表格或Excel，列出我们计划要做的事情。然后每周检查一下，这仍然是下个月的计划吗？

turns.en: Yeah, it's yeah, like very very we But even that one, I'm also still feeling, 'How can we even automate this more?' Because I never want it to feel like a tax when someone has to, you know, update the spreadsheet. So, this is actually yeah, like just yesterday we were chatting, 'Hey, how can we actually automate this?' Well, it's getting to that question, always ask yourselves, 'Can we actually automate this better?'
turns.zh: 是的，非常非常……但即使这样，我仍然在想，'我们怎么能进一步自动化呢？'因为我从不希望当有人需要更新表格时，这变成一种负担。所以，实际上，就在昨天我们还在聊，'嘿，我们怎么才能真正自动化这个？'嗯，这就是要问的问题，总是问自己，'我们能更好地自动化这个吗？'

turns.en: Like my PM brain is like, that's so like how could you not do something like that?
turns.zh: 我的产品经理大脑在想，这怎么可能不做呢？

turns.en: Okay, here's what we're thinking for the next month. Let's just check it once a week. Let's make sure this is like it's hard to imagine that not happening. Um and you don't have a lot of items on the spreadsheet is what I'm hearing also.
turns.zh: 好的，这是我们下个月的想法。我们每周检查一次。确保这……很难想象不这样做。而且我听说你的表格上项目也不多。

turns.en: Yeah, but we really try to focus. So, we will share out like here's what we think are the highest priorities. And again, for that agency of given the priorities, then everybody's like their kind of like item for how they think addresses those priorities.
turns.zh: 是的，但我们真的努力聚焦。所以我们会分享我们认为的最高优先级。然后，对于那个机构，给定这些优先级，每个人都有自己的项目，他们认为如何解决这些优先级。

turns.en: Is there anything that's like here's for the next like 6 months bigger bets kind of stuff or is it just like let's just think 1 month ahead?
turns.zh: 有没有像未来六个月的大赌注之类的东西？还是我们只考虑未来一个月？

turns.en: I like so we'll usually definitely there's themes of where we think the work and so we'll definitely like um and actually the whole team will bring everyone together uh like every 6 months. So there we'll usually kick off like some themes but then it's really the making sure we keep the pulse of what because again like even though themes change you know so fast when uh the landscape changes.
turns.zh: 我喜欢这样。我们通常会有一些主题，关于我们认为工作方向在哪里。实际上，整个团队每六个月会聚在一起。那时我们会启动一些主题，但关键是保持对变化的感知，因为即使主题变化很快，当格局改变时。

turns.en: All right, let's pull up the spreadsheet and let's take a look.
turns.zh: 好的，让我们打开表格看看。

turns.en: Oh man. Man, this whole podcast could have been just talking about this planning stuff they do. Okay, I'm going to have to find someone else to talk about this because this is so interesting just how y'all plan. Uh yeah, okay. Well, Fiona, with that we reached our very exciting lightning round. I've got five questions for you. Are you ready?
turns.zh: 哦，天哪。这整个播客本来可以只聊你们做的这些规划。好吧，我得找别人来聊这个，因为你们的规划方式太有趣了。嗯，好的。那么，Fiona，我们到了非常刺激的快问快答环节。我有五个问题要问你。准备好了吗？

turns.en: Ready.
turns.zh: 准备好了。

turns.en: Okay, first question. What are two or three books that you find yourself recommending most to other people?
turns.zh: 好的，第一个问题。你经常向别人推荐哪两三本书？

turns.en: Ooh, I will say for fiction actually two authors I recommend to everyone, Margaret Atwood and Haruki Murakami. Um those are just two like like I mean I grew up in Canada so I read a lot of Atwood growing up but her books are fascinating because it's almost like speculative fiction of you can kind of squint and say okay could this actually you know happen to a society. So I love her take on speculative fiction and Murakami I love his magical realism style. It's um but then the one book so there are authors but the one book I always recommend to everyone to read at least once a year or you know um The Little Prince. I like I think I'm sure we probably all read it at some point in our lives but I think it's a I read it at least once a year. It just you know, to remind me to think about like kind of like what's truly important.
turns.zh: 哦，对于小说，我实际上会推荐两位作家给所有人：玛格丽特·阿特伍德和村上春树。嗯，就是这两位。我在加拿大长大，所以小时候读了很多阿特伍德的作品，但她的书很迷人，因为几乎像是思辨小说，你可以眯起眼睛说，好吧，这真的可能发生在一个社会上吗？所以我喜欢她对思辨小说的处理。村上春树，我喜欢他的魔幻现实主义风格。嗯，但有一本书，虽然我推荐的是作家，但有一本书我总是推荐给每个人，至少每年读一次，那就是《小王子》。我想我们可能都在人生的某个时刻读过它，但我认为它……我每年至少读一次。它只是提醒我去思考什么才是真正重要的。

turns.en: Wow. That doesn't come up a bunch. Okay. I love it. Uh favorite recent movie or TV show you have really enjoyed?
turns.zh: 哇。这个不常见。好的，我喜欢。嗯，最近你非常喜欢的电影或电视剧是什么？

turns.en: I haven't watched TV shows.
turns.zh: 我没看电视剧。

turns.en: That's very common across Anthropic people I have on the podcast. Common theme.
turns.zh: 这在播客里我采访过的Anthropic员工中很常见。共同主题。

turns.en: But I will share with you what I always have downloaded on my phone so that if I'm on the airplane. So there's three movies I always have on my phone because I think they're just so fun to watch if I have time. One is Amelie. It's this French movie that oh my gosh, all of these movies are going to be very old by the way. It's going to be like vintage movies. But I loved Amelie. Super whimsical. So really highly recommend it to anyone that haven't seen it. It's you know, if you remember I told you I was going on a I thought I was going to be a visual artist. So when I was 16, my high school we took a trip a high school trip to Paris and that just I've so many memories of that and Amelie really captures the magic I felt at Paris. And the other two are Ghibli films. I love Spirited Away. That's um it's just such a like just such a I just love that story. I love the yeah, like I just love everything about Spirited Away. It's probably one of my favorite Ghibli films. And then the third was another Ghibli film um Nausicaa Valley of the Wind. And I think about this one quite a bit because if anyone asked me, 'Hey, how did you kind of think about you know, all these leadership traits?' I think what I watched that movie probably when I was eight or nine. And the heroine Nausicaa and how she seeing how she leads just left such a like um just left such a footprint in my heart I guess you could say that probably Nausicaa has inspired me a lot and a lot of my different leadership principles.
turns.zh: 但我会分享我手机里总是下载好的东西，这样我在飞机上就能看。有三部电影我手机里一直有，因为我觉得它们很有趣，有时间就可以看。一部是《天使爱美丽》。这是一部法国电影，哦天哪，顺便说一句，这些电影都很老了，算是经典电影。但我很喜欢《天使爱美丽》，非常奇幻。所以强烈推荐给没看过的人。你知道，如果你还记得我说过，我本来以为自己会成为视觉艺术家。我16岁时，高中组织了一次去巴黎的旅行，那给我留下了很多回忆，《天使爱美丽》真的捕捉到了我在巴黎感受到的魔力。另外两部是吉卜力电影。我喜欢《千与千寻》。嗯，它就是这样……我就是喜欢那个故事。我喜欢，是的，我喜欢《千与千寻》的一切。它可能是我最喜欢的吉卜力电影之一。第三部是另一部吉卜力电影，《风之谷》。我经常想到这部，因为如果有人问我，'嘿，你是怎么想到所有这些领导特质的？'我想我大概八九岁时看了这部电影。女主角娜乌西卡，看到她如何领导，在我心中留下了深深的印记，可以说娜乌西卡给了我很多启发，也影响了我许多不同的领导原则。

turns.en: Wow, what is that book called again?
turns.zh: 哇，那本书叫什么来着？

turns.en: Uh the movie's called Nausicaä of the Valley of the Wind, but it was actually based on a manga.
turns.zh: 嗯，电影叫《风之谷》，但它其实是基于漫画改编的。

turns.en: Valley of the Wind. So, it's like High Output Management Andy Grove, Valley of the Wind Nausicaä.
turns.zh: 《风之谷》。所以，就像安迪·格鲁夫的《高产出管理》和《风之谷》的娜乌西卡。

turns.en: That's right.
turns.zh: 没错。

turns.en: Two top management books. So cool. Okay. Uh do you have a favorite product you recently discovered that you really love? Could be an app, could be clothing, could be a gadget, could be kitchen equipment.
turns.zh: 两本顶级管理书。太酷了。好的。嗯，你最近有没有发现特别喜欢的产品？可以是应用、衣服、小工具、厨房用具。

turns.en: I'll share the product that I was reminded of how much has made a difference in my life recently. Uh because I've been just traveling a little bit and so I don't and I like to travel really light.
turns.zh: 我想分享一个产品，最近我意识到它对我的生活有多大影响。嗯，因为我最近经常旅行，而且我喜欢轻装出行。

turns.en: So, I use whatever shampoo and conditioner the hotel gives. And I forgot that I actually have one of their products—I promise this is not an infomercial—but Sweet Sisters Bodycare. It's a local business on Whidbey Island. The reason their product has made such a big difference in my life is that it's a full line of organic hair, body, and skin care. A few years ago, I started getting this rash on my nose that was really painful, actually bleeding. I couldn't figure out how to stop it. I tried not using any lotions, cut everything out on my face, but it still hurt. Then someone asked, 'What shampoo are you using?' I said, 'It's the same one I've used since I was a teenager.' They said, 'Maybe your body has developed an allergy to the chemicals in it.' I was like, 'What?' So I found this organic shampoo. Lo and behold, I used their shampoo—because you don't think that when you wash your hair, it goes over the rest of your body. Since then, I've switched everything to Sweet Sisters. I was recently reminded of how important this is because after a week of hotel shampoo, I started having skin reactions again. I thought, 'Maybe I should get travel-size bottles to bring with me.'
turns.zh: 所以，我都是用酒店提供的洗发水和护发素。我忘了其实我有一款他们的产品——我保证这不是广告——Sweet Sisters Bodycare。这是惠德比岛的一家本地企业。他们的产品之所以对我的生活产生巨大影响，是因为它是一整套有机头发、身体和护肤产品。几年前，我鼻子上开始长皮疹，非常疼，甚至流血。我完全不知道该怎么阻止。我试过不用任何乳液，脸上什么都不涂，但还是很疼。然后有人问我：‘你用什么洗发水？’我说：‘我从十几岁开始就一直用同一种。’他们说：‘也许你的身体对里面的化学物质产生了过敏。’我当时想：‘什么？’于是我找到了这款有机洗发水。果然，用了他们的洗发水——因为你不会想到洗头的时候水会流到身体其他部位。从那以后，我把所有东西都换成了Sweet Sisters。最近我又想起了这件事有多重要，因为用了一周酒店洗发水后，我的皮肤又开始有反应了。我想：‘也许我应该买旅行装瓶子随身带着。’

turns.en: This is an awesome pick. I love local business picks, even big bonus points for that. By the way, this travel you're doing—just for folks that may not know—there are these Code with Claude events happening all over the world. I went to the one in SF. There was one in London. You're going to one in Tokyo. Is that the end of it, or is there more after that?
turns.zh: 这个推荐太棒了。我喜欢本地企业的推荐，加分不少。顺便说一下，你这次旅行——可能有些人不知道——世界各地都在举办Code with Claude活动。我去了旧金山的那场，伦敦也有一场。你要去东京的那场。这是最后一站吗，还是之后还有更多？

turns.en: Tokyo is next week, and that's the last leg of the trip.
turns.zh: 东京是下周，那是这次旅行的最后一站。

turns.en: And then probably more in the future. So cool. I love that that's happening. Okay, two more questions. Do you have a favorite life motto that you find yourself coming back to often in work or in life?
turns.zh: 然后未来可能还会有更多。太酷了。我很高兴看到这些活动。好的，还有两个问题。你有没有最喜欢的人生格言，在工作或生活中经常用到？

turns.en: In work, I really love to remind folks to keep it simple. What is the thing you're really trying to do well, and focus on that? Keep it simple, because I think sometimes we can overthink. It's always a good mantra. In life, probably: in a world where you can be anything, be kind.
turns.zh: 在工作中，我真的很喜欢提醒大家保持简单。你真正想做好的是什么？专注于那件事。保持简单，因为我觉得有时候我们会想太多。这是一个很好的座右铭。在生活中，可能是：在一个你可以成为任何人的世界里，要善良。

turns.en: Yeah. I love that one. We were at a Montessori school tour, and that's what the teacher had up on the wall.
turns.zh: 是的。我喜欢这句。我们去参观一所蒙台梭利学校时，老师就把这句话挂在墙上。

turns.en: We have so many things going on that you never know what's happening in someone else's life, and one small act of kindness can make the biggest difference. For me, that was what I remembered from COVID. We were all working from home. I was in back-to-back meetings, and one-on-ones are really important to me because that time is usually also important for the other person. They might have things to discuss. So I always prioritize one-on-ones. During that time, my grandmother wasn't doing well; she was in a home in Canada, and because of COVID, I couldn't visit. Even my aunt and mom couldn't visit. It was rare that if they had a helper, we could do FaceTime, but you never know what time that will be. All of a sudden, I got a message from my aunt saying, 'Grandma can FaceTime at 12:00 p.m. today.' I thought, 'Oh no, there's this one-on-one I've been meaning to have.' I messaged my report and said, 'I'm really sorry. It's so last minute. Is it okay to reschedule?' He said, 'Yeah, totally. No problem at all.' For me, that was a small act of kindness. He didn't make it a big deal, but it made the biggest difference to me because I got to say hi to my grandmother on FaceTime.
turns.zh: 我们有太多事情要处理，你永远不知道别人生活中发生了什么，一个小小的善举就能带来巨大的改变。对我来说，这就是我在疫情期间记得的事情。我们都在家工作。我连续开会，一对一会议对我来说非常重要，因为那段时间通常对对方也很重要。他们可能有事情要讨论。所以我总是优先安排一对一会议。在那段时间，我祖母身体不好，她在加拿大的一家养老院，因为疫情，我无法去看望。甚至我姑姑和我妈妈也不能去。偶尔如果有护工帮忙，我们可以视频通话，但你永远不知道什么时候能通。突然，我姑姑发消息说：‘祖母今天中午12点可以视频。’我想：‘哦不，我正好有一个一直想开的一对一会议。’我给我的下属发消息说：‘真的很抱歉，这么临时通知。可以改期吗？’他说：‘没问题，完全没问题。’对我来说，这就是一个小小的善举。他没有把它当回事，但这对我影响巨大，因为我终于能和祖母视频通话了。

turns.en: I love that. Okay, final question. Boris Cherny, when I asked him about you, had this interesting insight: 'In very important meetings, you can often hear the click-clack of Fiona knitting in the background.' Maybe talk about what's going on there and what are a couple things you knitted recently?
turns.zh: 我喜欢这个故事。好的，最后一个问题。鲍里斯·切尔尼，当我问起你时，他有一个有趣的见解：‘在非常重要的会议上，你经常能听到菲奥娜在背景里织毛衣的咔嗒声。’能说说这是怎么回事吗？你最近织了些什么？

turns.en: Oh my gosh. Well, I knitted this top recently. You make your own clothing. Unreal. This is very meta for Claude Code building itself. You know, we're always building our clothing. Actually, there's a fun thing between knitting and programming because it's kind of like two stitches: knit and purl, so it's zero and one. Many concepts of stacks and queues actually apply. I'm kind of like a compiler, generating an executable when I knit. But I don't know about that. Actually, it was my grandma who taught me to knit when I was eight. I kind of mentioned her, and I go to that yarn shop. So every time I knit, I think of her. I always like to multitask, as you can see. I kick off multiple agents, so anytime I'm sitting—because I practice enough and got proficient, I don't have to look at what I'm doing—it's almost like how people have fidget spinners. Knitting is just something I do anytime I'm sitting still. I think, 'Oh, this is time to generate more knitting.' It's cute. I've got so much yarn that if I don't do this, I might have a slight yarn addiction problem.
turns.zh: 哦天哪。嗯，我最近织了这件上衣。你给自己做衣服，太不可思议了。这对于Claude Code自我构建来说非常元。你知道，我们总是在构建自己的衣服。实际上，编织和编程之间有一个有趣的联系，因为编织就像两种针法：下针和上针，所以是0和1。很多栈和队列的概念实际上都适用。我有点像编译器，编织的时候就在生成可执行文件。不过我也不确定。实际上，是我祖母在我八岁时教我织毛衣的。我提到过她，我常去那家毛线店。所以每次织毛衣，我都会想起她。我总喜欢一心多用，就像你看到的。我同时启动多个智能体，所以只要我坐着——因为我练习得足够多，已经很熟练了，不用看自己在做什么——这就像有些人玩指尖陀螺一样。织毛衣就是我坐着不动时做的事情。我想：‘哦，这是生成更多编织品的时间了。’很可爱。我有很多毛线，如果不织的话，我可能有点毛线上瘾的问题。

turns.en: I love this. When I asked Boris what he would do when AGI hits, when we don't have to work, he said he's going to make me. So I'm guessing your answer would be just knit and make beautiful clothing.
turns.zh: 我喜欢这个。当我问鲍里斯，当AGI到来、我们不用工作时他会做什么，他说他会让我做。所以我猜你的答案就是织毛衣，做漂亮的衣服。

turns.en: Oh my gosh, my dream is to actually open up a yarn store in my grandmother's name and create that community.
turns.zh: 哦天哪，我的梦想是以我祖母的名义开一家毛线店，创建那样的社区。

turns.en: And then co-work would help out, you automate everything.
turns.zh: 然后协同工作会帮忙，你把一切都自动化。

turns.en: That's right.
turns.zh: 没错。

turns.en: Especially invoicing. Oh my god. Fiona, you're awesome. It's just incredible the work that you and your team are doing just changing the world in such profound ways and there's no it's clear why it's growing so fast. So good job. Good job over there. Thank you for making time for this.
turns.zh: 尤其是发票处理。天哪，Fiona，你太棒了。你和你的团队正在以如此深刻的方式改变世界，这太不可思议了，而且很明显为什么它发展得这么快。干得好，干得好。谢谢你抽出时间。

turns.en: Well, I'm just really honestly lucky and humble that I get to work with such an amazing team. Like I know how lucky and I am I am and I'm so grateful. But thanks a lot for having me on the podcast as well. This was a lot of fun.
turns.zh: 嗯，我真的非常幸运和谦卑，能与如此出色的团队合作。我知道自己有多幸运，我充满感激。但也非常感谢你邀请我参加播客，这非常有趣。

turns.en: Uh to follow up on questions, where can folks find you online if you are online if they want to follow up on anything and how can listeners be useful to you?
turns.zh: 嗯，关于后续问题，如果大家想联系你，可以在网上哪里找到你？听众怎样才能帮到你？

turns.en: Ooh, uh so definitely I'm on LinkedIn and I'm sure most people have shared this already but would love feedback of what's going well, what's not going well. Um also any latent demand that you are all using that might be interesting use cases. You know, I had a friend message me recently to go, oh I'm using Claude to help me generate uh a building plan for my shed and he actually showed me his his shed, which was cool. So would would love to hear about that. And then yeah, maybe also, you know, we talked about reaching out across over there's someone whether a small business that you love or or someone that you feel like hasn't you know, as some of the listeners are super AI pill. Yeah, maybe take a time to hold somebody's hand to show what's AI might be able to help them with.
turns.zh: 哦，嗯，我肯定在 LinkedIn 上，我相信大多数人都已经分享过了。但我很希望得到反馈，哪些做得好，哪些不好。嗯，还有你们正在使用的任何潜在需求，可能是有趣的用例。你知道，最近有个朋友给我发消息说，哦，我正在用 Claude 帮我生成一个棚屋的建筑平面图，他还给我看了他的棚屋，很酷。所以我很想听听这些。然后，是的，也许还有，你知道，我们谈到过联系那些你喜爱的小企业，或者你觉得还没有接触过 AI 的人，因为有些听众是超级 AI 达人。是的，也许花点时间手把手教他们，展示 AI 能如何帮助他们。

turns.en: Such a good one. That is such a good answer to this question. Especially coming from you, Fiona. This was awesome. Thank you so much for being here.
turns.zh: 说得太好了。这个回答非常棒，尤其是出自你之口，Fiona。太棒了。非常感谢你来到这里。

turns.en: Thanks so much for having me, Lenny.
turns.zh: 非常感谢你的邀请，Lenny。

turns.en: Bye everyone. Thank you so much for listening. If you found this valuable, you can subscribe to the show on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, or your favorite podcast app. Also, please consider giving us a rating or leaving a review as that really helps other listeners find the podcast. You can find all past episodes or learn more about the show at lennyspodcast.com. See you in the next episode.
turns.zh: 大家再见。非常感谢收听。如果你觉得本期有价值，可以在 Apple Podcasts、Spotify 或你喜欢的播客应用上订阅节目。另外，请考虑给我们评分或留下评论，这真的能帮助其他听众找到这个播客。你可以在 lennyspodcast.com 找到所有过往剧集或了解更多节目信息。下期再见。
